steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The claim has two requirements: the quotation must use figurative language, and it must connect the urban setting of Washington, DC with the rural South of the narrator's past. Any choice missing either element is wrong.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — "The [train] engines of this valley have a whistle, the echoes of which sound like iterated gasps and sobs. I always think of them as crude music.": Uses a simile ("crude music"), but the setting is a valley — this does not explicitly connect DC to the rural South. ✗
  • Choice B — "I saw the dawn steal over Washington [DC].": "Steal" is mildly figurative, but the line describes only DC and makes no connection to the rural South. ✗
  • Choice C — "And when the wind is from the South, soil of my homeland falls like a fertile shower upon the lean streets of [Washington, DC].": Contains a simile ("falls like a fertile shower") and explicitly links the rural South ("soil of my homeland") to urban DC ("lean streets of [Washington, DC]"). Both requirements are met. ✓
  • Choice D — "A band in one of the buildings a fair distance off was playing a march. I wished they would stop. Their playing was like a tin spoon in one's mouth.": Uses a simile, but the setting is urban only — no connection to the rural South is made. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
